title: Getting Started with C++
content:
### Installing a C++ Compiler

To write and run C++ programs, you need a compiler. Here are the most common options:

| Compiler | Platform | Command |
|----------|----------|---------|
| GCC (g++) | Linux, macOS, Windows (MinGW) | `g++ program.cpp -o program` |
| Clang | macOS, Linux | `clang++ program.cpp -o program` |
| MSVC | Windows (Visual Studio) | `cl program.cpp` |

---

### Recommended IDEs

- **Visual Studio** (Windows) - Full-featured IDE with MSVC
- **VS Code** (Cross-platform) - Lightweight with C++ extensions
- **CLion** (Cross-platform) - JetBrains IDE for C/C++
- **Code::Blocks** (Cross-platform) - Free and open-source

---

### Your First C++ Program

Create a file called `hello.cpp` with the following code:

```cpp
#include <iostream>

int main() {
  std::cout << "Hello, World!" << std::endl;
  return 0;
}
```

To compile and run:
```bash
g++ hello.cpp -o hello
./hello
```

---

> **Key Point:** The `main()` function is the entry point of every C++ program. Execution always begins here.

quiz.question: Which function is the entry point of a C++ program?
